fingerprints.method_out_of_state: IdentoGO Out-of-State Live Scan (adds ~$39.95 fee) OR FD-258 hard card via mail (tip: moisturize hands for days prior to reduce smudge rejects)

seo.title: Illinois Dietitian License Reciprocity - How to Transfer Your License (2026)
seo.description: Already licensed as a dietitian? Here's whether your license transfers to Illinois - compact status, endorsement steps, fees, and processing time.
seo.keywords: dietitian license reciprocity illinois, how to transfer dietitian license to illinois, dietitian compact illinois, illinois dietitian endorsement application

board.name: Dietetic and Nutrition Services Practice Board
board.parent_dept: Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation (IDFPR)
board.mailing_address:
IDFPR
ATTN: Dietetic and Nutrition Services

Springfield, IL 62791
board.physical_address:
IDFPR Chicago Office
555 West Monroe Street, 5th Floor
Chicago, IL 60661

license_types.title: Licensed Dietitian Nutritionist (LDN)
license_types.description: The unified credential to provide dietetic and nutrition services in the state of Illinois. Overseen by the IDFPR Dietetic and Nutrition Services Practice Board.

steps.title: Meet Educational & Experience Prerequisites
steps.description: Complete a baccalaureate or higher degree (Master's required for CDR exam as of 2024), alongside a documented 900 hours of continuous supervised practice experience.

steps.title: Pass National Examination
steps.description: Successfully pass the Commission on Dietetic Registration (CDR) Registered Dietitian Nutritionist examination.

steps.title: Submit the IDFPR Application via CORE
steps.description: Submit a new licensure application online using the state's new CORE licensing portal, and pay the non-refundable $100 application fee.

documents.detail: Degree-granting institution transcripts must be sent directly to the IDFPR or uploaded securely through the CORE portal.
Primary source verification of your CDR exam score, typically routed electronically by the credentialing agency.
Documentation proving the completion of the mandatory 900-hour supervised practice requirement.

provisional_text: Illinois allows candidates who have submitted a completed application to be authorized to practice for up to six months while their application is pending final approval by the IDFPR.
reciprocity_text: Applicants licensed in other jurisdictions may file an Endorsement application through the IDFPR, provided the requirements of the other state were substantially equivalent to Illinois' requirements at the time of original licensure, and the license remains in active and good standing.

faqs.question: What is the fee for an Illinois LDN license?
faqs.answer: The IDFPR requires a flat $100 fee for the initial application.

faqs.question: When does the Illinois LDN license expire?
faqs.answer: All individual dietitian nutritionist licenses expire simultaneously on October 31 of every odd-numbered year.

faqs.question: How many continuing education hours are required to renew my IL license?
faqs.answer: You must complete 30 hours of continuing education (CE) during each two-year renewal cycle prior to the October 31 expiration date.

faqs.question: Does Illinois have reciprocity for dietitians?
faqs.answer: No interstate compact currently covers dietitian practice in Illinois. To work in Illinois, apply for licensure by endorsement through the state board.

faqs.question: How long does it take to transfer my dietitian license to Illinois?
faqs.answer: Most endorsement applications take 4 to 6 Weeks. If you qualify for an active compact privilege, there may be no separate wait to start practicing.

faqs.question: Can I start working in Illinois while my endorsement application is processing?
faqs.answer: A temporary or provisional credential is available while the full endorsement application is pending.

reciprocity.notes: Applicants licensed in other jurisdictions may file an Endorsement application through the IDFPR, provided the requirements of the other state were substantially equivalent to Illinois' requirements at the time of original licensure, and the license remains in active and good standing.
